1: A revelation of God: "Do you indeed decree what is right, you rulers? Do you judge human beings with justice?" Psalm 58 opens with a direct challenge to unjust human governance — the rhetorical question that exposes corrupt power. This is the prophetic function of the Psalter at its most confrontational. Acts 17:31 grounds all human accountability to divine justice: "For he has set a day when he will judge the world with justice by the man he has appointed." Christ is the appointed judge before whom all human "rulers" will give account for whether they "decreed what is right." The Psalm's challenge to unjust rulers anticipates the final judgment through Christ.
2: A revelation of God: "No — in your heart you devise wrongs; your hands administer violence throughout the land." The gap between the judicial office (decreeing right) and the actual conduct (devising wrongs, administering violence) is the corruption Psalm 58 exposes. Romans 2:21-23 applies similar logic to those who teach the law while breaking it. Jesus applies it directly: Matthew 23:3, "So you must be careful to do everything they tell you. But do not do what they do, for they do not practice what they preach." The devising-of-wrongs in the heart while holding judicial power is the hypocrisy Christ confronts most consistently in the Gospels.
3: A revelation of God: "The wicked are alienated from the womb; from their very birth they go astray, speaking lies." The radical origin of human sin — alienation from birth, going astray from the womb — is the Psalm's assertion of what later theology calls original sin. Psalms 51:5 parallels this: "Surely I was sinful at birth, sinful from the time my mother conceived me." Romans 3:23: "for all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God." The universal sinfulness from birth that Psalms 58:3 asserts is the condition that makes the Incarnation necessary — the one born without alienation (Luke 1:35: "the holy one to be born will be called the Son of God") to rescue those born alienated.
4: A direct citation: "Their venom is like the venom of a snake; they are like the deaf cobra that blocks its ear." Paul cites the next clause ("their throat is an open grave" from Psalms 5:9) and the viper-venom image (combined from Psalms 58:4 and Psalms 140:3) in Romans 3:13: "their throats are open graves; with their tongues they practice deceit; the poison of vipers is on their lips." Paul uses this cluster of Psalm-texts to prove universal human sinfulness. The snake-venom of Psalms 58:4 becomes part of Paul's sweeping indictment of all humanity before Christ — and the antidote to viper-venom is the one "lifted up like a snake in the desert" (John 3:14), whose lifting reverses the venom's effect.
5: A revelation of God: "that will not listen to the voice of those who charm, no matter how skillfully they do it." The deaf cobra that will not hear the charmer — implacable in its hostility — is the Psalm's image for those who resist every appeal. Luke 16:31: "If they do not listen to Moses and the Prophets, they will not be convinced even if someone rises from the dead." The one who rises from the dead is precisely the "charmer" the implacable reject: Christ risen still does not convince those who have closed their ears. The deafness of Psalms 58:5 is the deafness of those who reject even the resurrection.
6: A shadow: "Smash their teeth in their mouths, O God; tear out the fangs of the young lions, O LORD!" The imprecatory prayer against the weapon-teeth of the violent is the Psalm's justice cry. Christ's enemies are described in lion-and-fang imagery throughout the Psalter (Psalms 7:2; Psalms 22:13; Psalms 57:4). The divine smashing of their teeth is the eschatological defeat of all hostile power: Revelation 19:15 (the sword from Christ's mouth conquers the armies), and the final defeat of all enemies "under his feet" (1 Corinthians 15:25-26). The prayer for tooth-smashing in Psalms 58:6 is the raw form of the eschatological victory that Christ achieves.
7: A shadow: "Let them drain away like water flowing downhill; when they draw their bows, let their arrows fall harmless." The draining of violent power — arrows rendered harmless — is the imprecatory prayer's image of judgment defanging evil. Ephesians 6:16: "take up the shield of faith, with which you can extinguish all the flaming arrows of the evil one." The arrows that fall harmless in Psalms 58:7 are the arrows extinguished by the shield of faith — a defense available only through the victory Christ has already won over the powers.
8: A shadow: "Let them be like a snail that dissolves as it goes, like a stillborn child that never sees the light of the sun." The imagery of futility — the snail that disappears in its own trail, the stillborn that never sees the light — is the Psalm's most vivid picture of those who never participate in the life of the coming age. John 3:19-20: "Light has come into the world, but people loved darkness instead of light because their deeds were evil... everyone who does evil hates the light." Those who "never see the light of the sun" in Psalms 58:8 are those who reject the Light of the world (John 8:12) — self-chosen non-participation in the resurrection's light.
9: A shadow: "Before your cooking pots have felt the heat of the thornwood — both green and dry — he will whirl them away." The sudden divine judgment that comes before human preparations are complete — before the pot even heats — is the Psalm's imagery of the unexpectedness of divine action against the violent. Luke 12:20: "You fool! This very night your life will be demanded from you." The divine whirlwind that sweeps away the wicked before they complete their plans is the eschatological judgment that Christ both announces and will execute at his return (Matthew 24:27: "For as lightning comes from the east and is visible even in the west, so will be the coming of the Son of Man").
10: A revelation of God: "The righteous will rejoice when they see justice carried out; they will wash their feet in the blood of the wicked." The eschatological rejoicing of the righteous at divine justice — a visceral image of vindication — must be understood within ANE warfare imagery. Revelation 19:2: "for true and just are his judgments. He has condemned the great prostitute who corrupted the earth by her adulteries. He has avenged on her the blood of his servants." The righteous rejoicing at divine justice in Psalms 58:10 is the heavenly worship of Revelation 19 — the declaration that YHWH's judgments are "true and just" as the vindication of the martyred righteous.
11: A revelation of God: "Then people will say, 'There really is a reward for the righteous; truly there is a God who judges the earth.'" The eschatological confession — that divine governance of the earth is real, that righteousness is rewarded — is the conclusion the Psalm drives toward. Acts 17:31: "For he has set a day when he will judge the world with justice by the man he has appointed. He has given proof of this to everyone by raising him from the dead." The "God who judges the earth" of Psalms 58:11 acts through the one raised from the dead — Christ as the appointed judge whose resurrection proves the eschatological governance of YHWH is real.
